long_candidates.reason: 4 politicians buying MSFT: Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, Mcguire

decisions.reasoning: Strongest signal in the dataset: 4 politicians across both chambers (Warner, Boozman, Fetterman, McGuire) all buying MSFT within a ~6-week window. This constitutes a genuine cluster. Mark Warner sits on the Senate Intelligence Committee with direct oversight of tech/AI policy, adding meaningful committee relevance. Trade amounts are small but the breadth of participation across partisan lines (Fetterman is progressive, Boozman is conservative) reduces the chance this is purely a partisan/public-news reaction. Sentiment score is mildly positive (0.163) across 50 articles, suggesting no obvious public catalyst driving this — incremental insider signal conviction.
decisions.key_risk: Small trade amounts ($1K–$15K each) suggest this could be routine portfolio rebalancing or index-linked purchases rather than conviction trades. MSFT is also heavily covered publicly (AI/cloud narrative), so cluster could simply reflect the same public thesis.

decisions.reasoning: Only 2 politicians buying (Fetterman, Keating), and neither has a clear committee oversight role over banking/finance that would elevate this to a conflict-of-interest signal. Minimum trade amounts ($1K–$15K) are too small to signal high conviction. JPM is a large-cap financials bellwether often bought as a general market proxy. Without committee leverage or a larger cluster, this does not clear the bar for a BUY.
decisions.key_risk: Signal is too thin — only 2 actors with no confirmed committee oversight, and amounts are at the floor of the reporting range.

decisions.reasoning: Two senators (Boozman, Fetterman) buying GOOGL. Boozman is on the Senate Agriculture and Appropriations committees — limited direct tech oversight. Fetterman is not a primary tech committee member. The cluster is minimal (2 actors) and the amounts are at the minimum reporting threshold. GOOGL is under ongoing antitrust scrutiny, which could be a public-news-driven trade rather than insider signal. Not enough conviction to BUY.
decisions.key_risk: Ongoing DOJ antitrust proceedings against GOOGL represent a material regulatory headwind that could override any soft insider signal here.

decisions.reasoning: Constellation Energy (CEG) is a nuclear/power name with direct policy relevance — AI data center power demand and nuclear energy policy are active legislative topics. Both Boozman (Senate Appropriations, Energy subcommittee exposure) and Warner (Senate Intelligence, with jurisdiction touching energy security) buying within 7 days of each other is a meaningful cluster for a less-followed name. CEG is not a reflexive index buy — these are more purposeful, sector-specific trades. Signal is moderate but elevated relative to generic large-caps.
decisions.key_risk: Only 2 actors and minimum trade amounts. CEG has had a strong run tied to AI power demand narrative, so this could be a public-thesis trade rather than insider information.

decisions.reasoning: AAPL has simultaneous BUY signals (Warner, McGuire) and SELL signals (Capito, Taylor) — a directly contradictory dataset. Two politicians buying and two politicians selling within the same reporting window creates signal cancellation. This is a classic case of routine portfolio management across a mega-cap household name, not coordinated insider-informed trading in either direction. Neither the long nor the short side has conviction.
decisions.key_risk: Contradictory signals from politicians of differing committee backgrounds and chambers make it impossible to extract a directional edge. Any position is essentially noise.

decisions.reasoning: Two politicians selling AT&T (Fetterman, Gottheimer). Fetterman's sale is dated 2026-04-03 — over 6 weeks before Gottheimer's May 20 sale — weakening the 'cluster' thesis significantly. AT&T is a slow-moving telecom with well-known public headwinds (debt load, cord-cutting, fiber buildout costs). Neither politician sits on a telecom-specific oversight committee per available data. With shorts carrying unlimited downside risk, this weak and temporally dispersed signal does not justify opening a short position.
decisions.key_risk: Insufficient clustering (trades 47 days apart), no confirmed committee oversight, and telecom sector has shown stabilization — shorting here risks being caught in a mean-reversion rally.

decisions.reasoning: Two House members (Evans, McCormick) selling AMT (American Tower REIT) within a ~2-day window. While the timing is tight, neither Evans nor McCormick sits on a committee with direct oversight of REITs or telecom infrastructure. AMT has been under pressure from rising interest rates (REIT sensitivity) — this is a well-publicized public thesis, not obvious insider knowledge. With only 2 actors, no committee signal, and a plausible public explanation, the short signal does not meet the conviction threshold. Shorts have unlimited downside; this is not the place to deploy capital.
decisions.key_risk: REITs are rate-sensitive and AMT's weakness is already priced into public discourse. Shorting here risks being squeezed if rate expectations shift dovish.
